question: Explain the key differences between an array and a linked list. Discuss one scenario where an array would be the preferred data structure and one scenario where a linked list would be preferred.
expectedPoints: Arrays provide constant-time access using an index but have fixed size and expensive insertions/deletions.
Linked lists have dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions but require sequential access, leading to linear-time lookups.

An array is preferred for frequent random access (e.g., storing pixel data for an image). A linked list is preferred for frequent insertions/deletions from the middle (e.g., a music playlist where songs are frequently added/removed).

question: Describe the process of inserting a new node with the value '25' into its correct position in the following sorted singly linked list: head -> 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40.
expectedPoints: Traverse the list starting from the head, comparing each node's value to 25.
Stop the traversal when the current node's value (30) is greater than 25, or the end of the list is reached.
Create the new node with value 25. Set the new node's 'next' pointer to the node containing 30.
Update the 'next' pointer of the previous node (the node containing 20) to point to the new node.

question: Compare and contrast the time complexities for the core operations (insertion, deletion, search, access) of Arrays, Linked Lists, Binary Search Trees (BSTs), and Hash Tables. Discuss the best-case and worst-case scenarios for each, explaining what causes the worst-case performance for BSTs and Hash Tables.
expectedPoints: Arrays: O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion/search; Linked Lists: O(n) for all operations except insertion/deletion at head (O(1)).
Balanced BST: O(log n) for insertion, deletion, and search. Worst-case (degenerated to a linked list) is O(n) due to unbalanced insertions.
Hash Tables: O(1) average case for insertion, deletion, search. Worst-case O(n) occurs due to many hash collisions, effectively turning buckets into linked lists.

The summary should contrast the constant-time access of arrays with the efficient average-case operations of hash tables and the ordered nature and efficient average-case of balanced BSTs versus the sequential access of linked lists.
